volumeTitle: Persians. Seven against Thebes. Suppliants. Prometheus Bound
label: Prometheus Bound (Section 332 - Section 517)

sections.label: Section 332
sections.original: ζηλῶ σ ὁθούνεκ ἐκτὸς αἰτίας κυρεῖς ,
sections.translation: I envy you because you have escaped blame for having dared to share with me in my troubles . [*] So now leave me alone and let it not concern you .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: πάντως γὰρ οὐ πείσεις [*] νιν : οὐ γὰρ εὐπιθής . [*]
sections.translation: Do what you want , you cannot persuade him ; for he is not easy to persuade . Beware that you do not do yourself harm by the mission you take .

sections.label: Section 337
sections.original: πολλῷ γ ἀμείνων τοὺς πέλας φρενοῦν ἔφυς
sections.translation: In truth , you are far better able to admonish others than yourself . It is by fact , not by hearsay , that I judge .

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: αὐχῶ γὰρ αὐχῶ τήνδε δωρεὰν ἐμοὶ
sections.translation: So do not hold back one who is eager to go . For I am confident , yes , confident , that Zeus will grant me this favor , to free you from your sufferings .

sections.label: Section 343
sections.original: προθυμίας γὰρ οὐδὲν ἐλλείπεις . ἀτὰρ
sections.translation: I thank you for all this and shall never cease to thank you ; in zeal you lack nothing , but do not trouble yourself ; for your trouble will be vain and

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: ἐμοὶ πονήσεις , εἴ τι καὶ πονεῖν θέλεις .
sections.translation: not helpful to me — if indeed you want to take the pain . No , keep quiet and keep yourself clear of harm . For even if I am in sore plight , I would not wish affliction on everyone else . No , certainly , no ! since , besides , I am distressed by the fate

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: τείρουσ Ἄτλαντος , ὃς πρὸς [*] ἑσπέρους τόπους
sections.translation: of my brother Atlas , who , towards the west , stands bearing on his shoulders the pillar of heaven and earth , a burden not easy for his arms to grasp . Pity moved me , too , at the sight of the earth-born dweller of the Cilician caves curbed by violence , that destructive monster

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: ἑκατογκάρανον πρὸς βίαν χειρούμενον
sections.translation: of a hundred heads , impetuous Typhon . He withstood all the gods , hissing out terror with horrid jaws , while from his eyes lightened a hideous glare , as though he would storm by force the sovereignty of Zeus .

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: ἀλλ ἦλθεν αὐτῷ Ζηνὸς ἄγρυπνον βέλος ,
sections.translation: But the unsleeping bolt of Zeus came upon him , the swooping lightning brand with breath of flame , which struck him , frightened , from his loud-mouthed boasts ; then , stricken to the very heart , he was burnt to ashes and his strength blasted from him by the lightning bolt .

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: καὶ νῦν ἀχρεῖον καὶ παράορον [*] δέμας
sections.translation: And now , a helpless and a sprawling bulk , he lies hard by the narrows of the sea , pressed down beneath the roots of Aetna ; while on the topmost summit Hephaestus sits and hammers the molten ore . There , one day , shall burst forth

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: ποταμοὶ πυρὸς δάπτοντες ἀγρίαις γνάθοις
sections.translation: rivers of fire , [*] with savage jaws devouring the level fields of Sicily , land of fair fruit — such boiling rage shall Typho , although charred by the blazing lightning of Zeus , send spouting forth with hot jets of appalling , fire-breathing surge .

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: σὺ δ οὐκ ἄπειρος , οὐδ ἐμοῦ διδασκάλου [*]
sections.translation: But you are not inexperienced , and do not need me to teach you . Save yourself , as you know best ; while I exhaust my present lot until the time comes when the mind of Zeus shall abandon its wrath .

sections.label: Section 379
sections.original: οὔκουν , Προμηθεῦ , τοῦτο γιγνώσκεις , ὅτι
sections.translation: Do you not know then , Prometheus , that

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: ὀργῆς νοσούσης εἰσὶν ἰατροὶ λόγοι ;
sections.translation: words are the physicians of a disordered temper ?

sections.label: Section 381
sections.original: ἐάν τις ἐν καιρῷ γε μαλθάσσῃ κέαρ
sections.translation: If one softens the soul in season , and does not hasten to reduce its swelling rage by violence .

sections.label: Section 383
sections.original: ἐν τῷ προθυμεῖσθαι δὲ καὶ τολμᾶν τίνα
sections.translation: What lurking mischief do you see when daring joins to zeal ? Teach me this .

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: μόχθον περισσὸν κουφόνουν τ εὐηθίαν .
sections.translation: Lost labor and thoughtless simplicity .

sections.label: Section 386
sections.original: ἔα με τῇδε τῇ νόσῳ νοσεῖν , ἐπεὶ
sections.translation: Leave me to be affected by this , since it is most advantageous , when truly wise , to be deemed a fool .

sections.label: Section 388
sections.original: ἐμὸν δοκήσει τἀμπλάκημ εἶναι τόδε .
sections.translation: This fault will be seen to be my own .

sections.label: Section 389
sections.original: σαφῶς μ ἐς οἶκον σὸς λόγος στέλλει πάλιν .
sections.translation: Clearly the manner of your speech orders me back home .

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: μὴ γάρ σε θρῆνος οὑμὸς εἰς ἔχθραν βάλῃ .
sections.translation: So that you won ’ t win enmity for yourself by lamenting for me .

sections.label: Section 391
sections.original: ἦ τῷ νέον θακοῦντι παγκρατεῖς ἕδρας ;
sections.translation: In the eyes of the one who is newly seated on his omnipotent throne ?

sections.label: Section 392
sections.original: τούτου φυλάσσου μή ποτ ἀχθεσθῇ κέαρ .
sections.translation: Beware lest the time come when his heart is angered with you .

sections.label: Section 393
sections.original: ἡ σή , Προμηθεῦ , συμφορὰ διδάσκαλος .
sections.translation: Your plight , Prometheus , is my instructor .

sections.label: Section 394
sections.original: στέλλου , κομίζου , σῷζε τὸν παρόντα νοῦν .
sections.translation: Go away , depart , keep your present purpose .

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: ὁρμωμένῳ μοι τόνδ ἐθώυξας λόγον .
sections.translation: Your urging meets my eagerness ; for my four-footed winged beast fans with his wings the smooth pathway of the air ; and truly he will be glad to rest his knees in his stall at home . Exit

sections.label: Section 1.399
sections.original: στένω σε τᾶς οὐλομένας τύχας , Προμηθεῦ :
sections.translation: I mourn your unfortunate fate , Prometheus .

sections.label: Section 1.400
sections.original: δακρυσίστακτα [*] δ ἀπ ὄσσων
sections.translation: Shedding from my eyes a coursing flood of tears I wet my tender cheeks with their moist streams . For Zeus , holding this unenviable power by self-appointed laws ,

sections.label: Section 1.405
sections.original: ὑπερήφανον [*] θεοῖς τοῖς
sections.translation: displays towards the gods of old an overweening spirit .

sections.label: Section 1.407
sections.original: πρόπασα δ ἤδη στονόεν λέλακε χώρα ,
sections.translation: Now the whole earth cries aloud in lamentation ; . . . lament the greatness of the glory of your time-hallowed honor ,

sections.label: Section 1.410
sections.original: συνομαιμόνων τε τιμάν ,
sections.translation: the honor that was yours and your brother ’ s ; and all mortals who make their dwelling place in holy Asia share the anguish of your most lamentable suffering ;

sections.label: Section 2.415
sections.original: Κολχίδος τε γᾶς ἔνοικοι
sections.translation: And those who dwell in the land of Colchis , the maidens fearless in fight ; and the Scythian multitude that inhabits the most remote region of the earth bordering the Maeotic lake ;

sections.label: Section 2.420
sections.original: † Ἀραβίας [*] τ ἄρειον ἄνθος ,
sections.translation: And the warlike flower of Arabia , which hold the high-cragged citadel near the Caucasus , a hostile host that roars among the sharp-pointed spears .

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: † μόνον δὲ [*] πρόσθεν [ ἄλλον [*] ] ἐν πόνοις [*]
sections.translation: One other Titan god before this I have seen in distress , enthralled in torment by adamantine bonds — Atlas , pre-eminent in mighty strength , who moans as he supports

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: νώτοις ὑποστενάζει .†
sections.translation: the vault of heaven on his back .

sections.label: Section 431
sections.original: βοᾷ δὲ πόντιος κλύδων
sections.translation: The waves of the sea utter a cry as they fall , the deep laments , the black abyss of Hades rumbles in response , and the streams of pure-flowing rivers

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: στένουσιν ἄλγος οἰκτρόν .
sections.translation: lament your piteous pain .

sections.label: Section 436
sections.original: μή τοι χλιδῇ δοκεῖτε μηδ αὐθαδίᾳ
sections.translation: No , do not think it is from pride or even from wilfulness that I am silent . Painful thoughts devour my heart as I behold myself maltreated in this way . And yet who else but I definitely assigned

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: τίς ἄλλος ἢ γὼ παντελῶς διώρισεν ;
sections.translation: their prerogatives to these upstart gods ? But I do not speak of this ; for my tale would tell you nothing except what you know . Still , listen to the miseries that beset mankind — how they were witless before and I made them have sense and endowed them with reason .

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: λέξω δέ , μέμψιν οὔτιν ἀνθρώποις ἔχων ,
sections.translation:
I will not speak to upbraid mankind but to set forth the friendly purpose that inspired my blessing .
First of all , though they had eyes to see , they saw to no avail ; they had ears , but they did not understand ; but , just as shapes in dreams , throughout their length of days ,

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: ἔφυρον εἰκῆ πάντα , κοὔτε πλινθυφεῖς
sections.translation: without purpose they wrought all things in confusion . They had neither knowledge of houses built of bricks and turned to face the sun nor yet of work in wood ; but dwelt beneath the ground like swarming ants , in sunless caves . They had no sign either of winter

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: οὔτ ἀνθεμώδους ἦρος οὔτε καρπίμου
sections.translation:
or of flowery spring or of fruitful summer , on which they could depend but managed everything without judgment , until I taught them to discern the risings of the stars and their settings , which are difficult to distinguish .
Yes , and numbers , too , chiefest of sciences ,

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: ἐξηῦρον αὐτοῖς , γραμμάτων τε συνθέσεις ,
sections.translation: I invented for them , and the combining of letters , creative mother of the Muses ’ arts , with which to hold all things in memory . I , too , first brought brute beasts beneath the yoke to be subject to the collar and the pack-saddle , so that they might bear in men ’ s stead their

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: γένοινθ , [*] ὑφ ἅρμα τ ἤγαγον φιληνίους
sections.translation:
heaviest burdens ; and to the chariot I harnessed horses and made them obedient to the rein , to be an image of wealth and luxury . It was I and no one else who invented the mariner ’ s flaxen-winged car that roams the sea .
Wretched that I am — such are the arts I devised

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: βροτοῖσιν , αὐτὸς οὐκ ἔχω σόφισμ ὅτῳ [*]
sections.translation: for mankind , yet have myself no cunning means to rid me of my present suffering .

sections.label: Section 472
sections.original: πέπονθας αἰκὲς [*] πῆμ : ἀποσφαλεὶς φρενῶν
sections.translation: You have suffered sorrow and humiliation . You have lost your wits and have gone astray ; and , like an unskilled doctor , fallen ill , you lose heart and cannot

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: εὑρεῖν ὁποίοις φαρμάκοις ἰάσιμος .
sections.translation: discover by which remedies to cure your own disease .

sections.label: Section 476
sections.original: τὰ λοιπά μου κλύουσα θαυμάσει πλέον ,
sections.translation: Hear the rest and you shall wonder the more at the arts and resources I devised . This first and foremost : if ever man fell ill , there was no defence — no healing food ,

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: οὐ χριστόν , οὔτε πιστόν , ἀλλὰ φαρμάκων
sections.translation: no ointment , nor any drink — but for lack of medicine they wasted away , until I showed them how to mix soothing remedies with which they now ward off all their disorders . And I marked out many ways by which they might read the future ,

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: κἄκρινα πρῶτος ἐξ ὀνειράτων ἃ χρὴ
sections.translation: and among dreams I first discerned which are destined to come true ; and voices baffling interpretation I explained to them , and signs from chance meetings . The flight of crook-taloned birds I distinguished clearly — which by nature are auspicious ,

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: εὐωνύμους τε , καὶ δίαιταν ἥντινα
sections.translation: which sinister — their various modes of life , their mutual feuds and loves , and their consortings ; and the smoothness of their entrails , and what color the gall must have to please

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: χολῆς λοβοῦ τε ποικίλην εὐμορφίαν :
sections.translation: the gods , also the speckled symmetry of the liver-lobe ; and the thigh-bones , wrapped in fat , and the long chine I burned and initiated mankind into an occult art . Also I cleared their vision to discern signs from flames , which were obscure before this .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: τοιαῦτα μὲν δὴ ταῦτ : ἔνερθε δὲ χθονὸς
sections.translation: Enough about these arts . Now as to the benefits to men that lay concealed beneath the earth — bronze , iron , silver , and gold — who would claim to have discovered them before me ? No one , I know full well , unless he likes to babble idly .

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: βραχεῖ δὲ μύθῳ πάντα [*] συλλήβδην μάθε ,
sections.translation: Hear the sum of the whole matter in the compass of one brief word — every art possessed by man comes from Prometheus .

sections.label: Section 507
sections.original: μή νυν βροτοὺς μὲν ὠφέλει καιροῦ πέρα ,
sections.translation: Do not benefit mortals beyond reason and disregard your own distress ; although , I am confident that you will be freed

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: λυθέντα μηδὲν μεῖον ἰσχύσειν Διός .
sections.translation: from these bonds and will have power in no way inferior to Zeus .

sections.label: Section 511
sections.original: οὐ ταῦτα ταύτῃ Μοῖρά πω τελεσφόρος
sections.translation: Not in this way is Fate , who brings all to fulfillment , destined to complete this course . Only when I have been bent by pangs and tortures infinite am I to escape my bondage . Skill is weaker by far than Necessity .

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: τίς οὖν ἀνάγκης ἐστὶν οἰακοστρόφος ;
sections.translation: Who then is the helmsman of Necessity ?

sections.label: Section 516
sections.original: Μοῖραι τρίμορφοι μνήμονές τ Ἐρινύες .
sections.translation: The three-shaped Fates and mindful Furies .

sections.label: Section 517
sections.original: τούτων ἄρα Ζεύς ἐστιν ἀσθενέστερος ;
sections.translation: Can it be that Zeus has less power than they do ?
